_meta.how_to_regress: npm run check:golden tool-finder  (dev backend up)
_meta.note: catalog parser quote-agnostic + categories array (was dropping 5 tools / [undefined]); max_tokens 2000. 2026-07-15: fixed a real bug — Tool Finder recommended itself as the top result (e.g. for 'Something broke and I don't know what to do'), which is always useless since the user is already using Tool Finder. Root cause: buildCatalog() fed the model its OWN entry (id 'ToolFinder') alongside every other tool, with no instruction to exclude it. Fixed by filtering 'ToolFinder' out of TOOL_CATALOG at build time (so the model never sees it as an option) plus a defensive second filter at response-validation time. GUARD: the vague-input case below MUST NOT return id:'ToolFinder' in recommendations — this is the single worst possible result and it doesn't require an exact wording match to check for, only its absence. 2026-07-15 (2nd pass, same day): fixed a second, more severe bug found while investigating the first — buildCatalog()'s field-extraction regex used a [^'"]+ character class that excludes BOTH quote characters even though tools.js only delimits a given string with ONE of them. Any title/tagline/description containing an apostrophe ('don't', 'it's', 'you're') got silently truncated mid-sentence right before it — 80 of ~121 catalog fields were affected. Example: BikeMedic's tagline is plain ('A trailside mechanic in your pocket', no apostrophe) but its fuller description ('...expert follow-up when standard repairs don't work') was being cut to '...standard repairs don' and, worse, catalogToString() only ever showed tagline OR description, never both — so BikeMedic's only catalog line shown to the model never mentioned bicycles at all. That's the direct cause of it claiming to cover 'bike, appliance, car, gear' for a vague query. Fixed: (1) new extractField() helper uses a backreference (['"]) ... \1 so only the ACTUAL opening delimiter closes the match, recovering full text for all 80 affected fields; (2) catalogToString() now joins tagline AND description when both exist and differ, so narrow tools have two chances to state their scope explicitly; (3) added an explicit anti-overreach rule to the system prompt (rule 8) as defense-in-depth even with fixed data. GUARD: the bike-scoped-hedge case below must contain 'bicycle' (or equivalent hedge) in BikeMedic's `why` for a vague query — the earlier bug produced 'physical object or mechanical item (bike, appliance, car, gear)' with no hedge at all. 2026-07-15 (3rd pass, same day): fixed the recommendation/no_perfect_fit boundary — rule 5 said to 'suggest which tool comes closest' without saying WHERE, so the model duplicated a wrong-domain tool into BOTH recommendations[] (which the frontend renders as a prominent 'Your best tool' card, singular header when length===1) AND no_perfect_fit (an honest caveat below it) — producing a contradictory UI: a big 'best tool' card for Bike Medic on a refrigerator query, with text underneath admitting it doesn't actually fit. Reproduced live: 'My refrigerator is not working right but I don't know what's wrong' returned BikeMedic as the sole card even after the scope-hedging fix (it was honestly hedged — 'While BikeMedic is designed for bicycles...' — but still shown as THE recommendation). Fixed by rewriting rule 5 and the no_perfect_fit schema description: true category gaps (no tool addresses the domain at all) must leave recommendations EMPTY and mention the closest tool only in no_perfect_fit prose, never both. Genuinely partial fits (e.g. CrisisPrioritizer/BrainDumpBuddy for the stress of something breaking) still populate recommendations normally — confirmed the fix didn't overcorrect into suppressing real partial matches. GUARD: the refrigerator case below must have an EMPTY recommendations array with BikeMedic mentioned only in no_perfect_fit.

input.problem: I have a huge messy list of unrelated small tasks and want to batch similar ones together to power through them

output.understanding: You've got decision fatigue and context-switching overhead from juggling unrelated tasks. You need a way to group them strategically so you can focus deeply on one category at a time and build momentum.

recommendations.title: Batch Flow
recommendations.why: This is built exactly for your situation—it takes your messy task list and groups similar tasks together, then helps you protect your focus while working through each batch. It minimizes the mental gear-shifting that kills productivity.
recommendations.what_to_do: Paste or list all your unrelated tasks, and let it categorize them by type (emails, admin, creative work, etc.) so you can power through one batch at a time.

recommendations.title: Task Avalanche Breaker
recommendations.why: If your task list feels overwhelming even after batching, this tool breaks each batch into micro-steps so you're not paralyzed by the size of the group. Works perfectly after BatchFlow to make each batch feel manageable.
recommendations.what_to_do: Once you've batched your tasks, take the largest or most daunting batch and break it into tiny, concrete first steps you can execute immediately.

output.workflow: Start with BatchFlow to organize your chaos into logical batches. Then, if any batch still feels overwhelming, use TaskAvalancheBreaker to convert it into micro-steps. This two-step approach turns 'huge messy list' into 'clear, executable batches' into 'small, doable steps.'

input.problem: Something broke and I don't know what to do

output.understanding: You're facing something broken (physical item, system, relationship, plan—unclear which) and feel stuck because you don't know the next step. You need someone to help you diagnose what's actually wrong and what to do about it.

recommendations.title: Task Avalanche Breaker
recommendations.why: When something breaks and you don't know where to start, overwhelm hits fast. This tool turns that 'I don't know what to do' feeling into micro-steps you can actually take—whether that's diagnosing the problem, calling someone, researching, or fixing it yourself.
recommendations.what_to_do: Describe what broke and how you're feeling about it—anxious, confused, stuck—and the tool will break it into your first three actions.

recommendations.title: Plain Talk — Document Analyst
recommendations.why: If what broke came with instructions, a warranty, a manual, or documentation, this tool cuts through jargon and gives you the exact facts plus what they mean for you. Saves you from misreading something critical.
recommendations.what_to_do: If you have any documentation about the broken item (manual, warranty, instructions, receipt), paste it and ask what it actually says about repair or replacement.

output.clarification: What broke? (A physical item? A relationship? A work project? A system/software?) Is it something you own, or are you responsible for fixing something you didn't break? This would help me recommend much more targeted tools—e.g., if it's a bike, plant, or appliance, I have specialist tools for those.

input.problem: My bike chain keeps falling off

output.understanding: Your bike chain is mechanically failing — it's either misaligned, stretched, or the derailleur needs adjustment. You need hands-on troubleshooting steps before you're stuck on the road or waste money at a shop.

recommendations.title: Bike Medic
recommendations.why: This is exactly what you need — interactive step-by-step bicycle troubleshooting with animated visual demos. It'll walk you through chain tension, derailleur alignment, and worn sprocket diagnostics, then tell you whether it's a DIY fix or shop-worthy.
recommendations.what_to_do: Describe what's happening when the chain falls off (during shifting, at certain gears, on bumps) and whether you've noticed any visible damage or misalignment.

output.clarification: Does the chain fall off during specific situations (certain gears, when you pedal hard, on bumps) or randomly? That detail helps Bike Medic narrow down whether it's a derailleur, chain wear, or alignment issue.

input.problem: Something broke and I don't know what to do

output.understanding: You've got something broken but don't know where to start — you might not even know what type of thing it is, or what the actual problem is, so you're stuck in diagnosis mode.

recommendations.title: Bike Medic
recommendations.why: If it's a bicycle, this is your trailside mechanic — interactive step-by-step fixes with visual demos and expert follow-up when standard repairs don't work.
recommendations.what_to_do: Describe what's broken or take a photo of the problem area, and BikeMedic will walk you through diagnosis and repair.

recommendations.title: Plant Rescue
recommendations.why: If it's a plant that's struggling or dying, this diagnoses the problem from symptoms or a photo and gives you a prioritized rescue plan.
recommendations.what_to_do: Upload a photo or describe what's wrong with the plant (yellowing, wilting, spots, etc.), and get a diagnosis with step-by-step recovery steps.

recommendations.title: Pet Weirdness Decoder
recommendations.why: If your pet is behaving strangely or seems off, this tells you whether it's quirky or something that needs a vet — crucial for knowing urgency.
recommendations.what_to_do: Describe your pet's weird behavior or symptom and get AI analysis to determine if it's harmless or needs veterinary attention.

recommendations.title: Recipe Chaos Solver
recommendations.why: If something went wrong while cooking — burnt sauce, missing ingredients, flat flavor — this is your kitchen 911 with instant rescue solutions.
recommendations.what_to_do: Describe what went wrong mid-cook and get smart substitutions, fixes, or rescue approaches.

output.no_perfect_fit: These tools cover the most common 'something broke' scenarios (bikes, plants, pets, recipes), but if your broken thing is something else entirely — an appliance, a relationship, a gadget, a plan — I'd need to know what to give you the right tool. There's no single general 'object troubleshooting' tool in the suite.
output.clarification: What actually broke? A bike, a plant, a pet, food, an appliance, a device, a plan, or something else? That'll let me point you to exactly the right tool.

input.problem: My refrigerator is not working right but I don't know what's wrong.

output.understanding: You've got a malfunctioning refrigerator but no clear sense of what's actually broken — whether it's temperature, ice maker, compressor noise, or something else. You need to diagnose the problem before you can decide if it's a DIY fix, a service call, or a replacement.
output.no_perfect_fit: There is no appliance repair or refrigerator diagnostic tool in the DeftBrain catalog. The closest tool by domain is BikeMedic (/BikeMedic), which handles interactive troubleshooting and step-by-step repair for bicycles — but it is specifically built for bikes and does not cover major appliances or refrigeration systems. For a refrigerator diagnosis, you'd need a specialized appliance repair tool that can walk you through symptom assessment, identify common failure modes (thermostat, compressor, door seal, defrost cycle), and guide you toward solutions.
output.clarification: To help you better in the future: What specific symptoms is your fridge showing? (e.g., not cooling at all, cooling but ice maker broken, making strange noises, leaking water, running constantly). That would help determine if there's an adjacent DeftBrain tool — like Decision Coach or a cost-analysis tool — that could help you decide between repair vs. replacement.
